mission.title: Install and Configure ClickHouse
mission.description: ClickHouse is a real-time analytics database management system designed for generating analytical data reports efficiently. It is ideal for scenarios requiring high-speed data processing and real-time analytics. Key trade-offs include the need for careful schema design and potential complexity in managing distributed setups.

steps.title: Add the ClickHouse Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official ClickHouse Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration.

```bash

helm repo add clickhouse https://clickhouse.com/helm/charts

```

> This adds the ClickHouse Helm repository.

steps.title: Install ClickHouse CRDs
steps.description:
This step installs the Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) required for ClickHouse to function properly in Kubernetes.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/master/helm/clickhouse/templates/crds.yaml

```

> This installs the necessary CRDs.

steps.title: Install ClickHouse using Helm
steps.description:
This command installs ClickHouse in the specified namespace with the desired version.

```bash

helm install clickhouse clickhouse/clickhouse --namespace clickhouse --create-namespace --version 26.2.4.23-stable

```

> This installs ClickHouse with the specified version.

steps.title: Create a ClickHouse table
steps.description:
This step creates a sample table in ClickHouse to verify the installation.

```bash

kubectl exec -it $(kubectl get pods --namespace clickhouse -l app=clickhouse-server -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}') -- clickhouse-client -q 'CREATE TABLE test (id UInt32, name String) ENGINE = MergeTree() ORDER BY id;'

```

> This creates a simple test table.
commands: kubectl exec -it $(kubectl get pods --namespace clickhouse -l app=clickhouse-server -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}') -- clickhouse-client -q 'CREATE TABLE test (id UInt32, name String) ENGINE = MergeTree() ORDER BY id;'

steps.title: Verify ClickHouse installation
steps.description:
This command checks if the ClickHouse server is running and the created table exists.

```bash

kubectl exec -it $(kubectl get pods --namespace clickhouse -l app=clickhouse-server -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}') -- clickhouse-client -q 'SHOW TABLES;'

```

> This lists the tables to verify the installation.
commands: kubectl exec -it $(kubectl get pods --namespace clickhouse -l app=clickhouse-server -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}') -- clickhouse-client -q 'SHOW TABLES;'

uninstall.title: Uninstall ClickHouse
uninstall.description:
This step removes ClickHouse and all associated resources. Be aware that this will result in data loss.

```bash

helm uninstall clickhouse --namespace clickhouse

kubectl delete namespace clickhouse

```

> This uninstalls ClickHouse from the specified namespace.
> This deletes the namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade ClickHouse
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you back up your data. This step will upgrade ClickHouse to the latest version.

```bash

kubectl exec -it $(kubectl get pods --namespace clickhouse -l app=clickhouse-server -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}') -- clickhouse-client -q 'BACKUP TABLE test TO 'backup_location';'

helm upgrade clickhouse clickhouse/clickhouse --namespace clickhouse --version 26.2.4.23-stable

kubectl exec -it $(kubectl get pods --namespace clickhouse -l app=clickhouse-server -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}') -- clickhouse-client -q 'SHOW TABLES;'

```

> Backup your existing table.
> This upgrades ClickHouse to the specified version.
> Verify that the tables are still present after the upgrade.
commands: kubectl exec -it $(kubectl get pods --namespace clickhouse -l app=clickhouse-server -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}') -- clickhouse-client -q 'BACKUP TABLE test TO 'backup_location';'
kubectl exec -it $(kubectl get pods --namespace clickhouse -l app=clickhouse-server -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}') -- clickhouse-client -q 'SHOW TABLES;'

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Cannot connect to ClickHouse server'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The ClickHouse server pod may not be running.

**Fix:**
Check the pod status with 'kubectl get pods --namespace clickhouse' and restart if necessary.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Table does not exist'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The specified table may not have been created or may have been deleted.

**Fix:**
Verify table creation with 'SHOW TABLES;' command.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Insufficient resources'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The ClickHouse pod may not have enough resources allocated.

**Fix:**
Increase the resource limits in the Helm values file and redeploy.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to create table'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The schema definition may be incorrect.

**Fix:**
Review the CREATE TABLE statement for errors.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for new MergeTree settings, improved query performance for large datasets.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated the old HTTP interface for queries.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from v25.x, ensure to review the new MergeTree settings for optimal performance.

resolution.summary: You should see the output of 'SHOW TABLES;' listing the 'test' table, confirming that ClickHouse is installed and operational.
codeSnippets: kubectl exec -it $(kubectl get pods --namespace clickhouse -l app=clickhouse-server -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}') -- clickhouse-client -q 'CREATE TABLE test (id UInt32, name String) ENGINE = MergeTree() ORDER BY id;'
kubectl exec -it $(kubectl get pods --namespace clickhouse -l app=clickhouse-server -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}') -- clickhouse-client -q 'SHOW TABLES;'

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with sufficient resources.
